pages_preview.text_preview: SIPS 2017 Sample Size and Effect Size Workshop Expected Power Worksheet Jeremy Biesanz July 30, 2017 jbiesanz@mail.ubc.ca. Notes: The fabs package will be submitted soon to CRAN. By the end of August there will be additional useful functions included such as the ability to determine expected power given a certain sample size for the future study (i.e., holding future sample size constant, estimate expected power for that future study) as well as the ability to BYOP (Bring Your Own Posterior) to the calculation such as that based on an informative prior. For instance, by incorporating meta-analytic data with the study effect size estimate you are focused on. Step 1 Download and install the fabs package from github if you have not done so already. These three lines, assuming you have already installed the devtools library in R, accomplish that task. library(devtools) install_github("jbiesanz/fabs") library(fabs) Step 2 Have fun! Evaluate a simple analysis. Run the code below and see how long it takes to provide an answer. Generally between 20 seconds and a minute depending on the computer. desired_ep_d(d = .50, n1=25, n2=25, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=0) Run the code again. Is the answer exactly the same? Most likely not as expected power is computed through simulation and stochastic approximation. Generally the answer will not vary meaningfully much across repeated runs. If reproducability is critical, you can always use use the set.seed() function. If precision is critical, you can set it up on a loop and average the results from 20 or more runs. That will often get precision down to 2 or 3 decimal places which is well beyond what is required for practical research. # Will always produce the same answer. set.seed(123) desired_ep_d(d = .50, n1=25, n2=25, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=0) Specific examples and problems. You submitted a manuscript where the primary effect of interest was r(198) = .32. The editor strongly requests a pre-registered replication before accepting the manuscript for publication. For the pre-registration what sample size is required to achieve adequate statistical power given the uncertainty associated with your observed effect size? desired_ep_r(r = 0.32, df = 198, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=0) You want to replicate a study that observed d=0.80 with n=15 in each group. What sample size will you need in the replication to have expected power of .80? How does this compare to the estimate from either G*Power or power.t.test? Note that the power.t.test() function in R allows you to do a traditional power analysis given a fixed parameter δ = delta/sd (where delta and sd are parameters that you input into the function). desired_ep_d(d = 0.80, n1=15, n2=15, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=0) power.t.test(delta = 0.80, sd = 1, sig.level=.05, power = .80, type="two.sample", alternative="two.sided")$n 1

You want to replicate a study that observed d=.40 with n=100 in each group. What sample size will you need in the replication to have expected power of .80? desired_ep_d(d = .40, n1=100, n2=100, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=0) Given an observed effect size of r=.21 with df=80, what sample size is required to have expected statistical power of .80? desired_ep_r(r = 0.21, df = 80, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=0) Now consider adding the publication bias filter to the above question. How much does the answer change? desired_ep_r(r = 0.21, df = 80, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=1) ** When do expected power calculations yield required sample sizes smaller than traditional power calcula- tions?** Consider a small and very uncertain effect size estimate d = 0.10 with n=10 in each group. What sample size will you need in the replication to have expected power of .80? How does this to power.t.test? desired_ep_d(d = 0.15, n1=15, n2=15, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=0) power.t.test(delta = 0.15, sd = 1, sig.level=.05, power = .80, type="two.sample", alternative="two.sided")$n Why does expected power yield a smaller required sample size? To understand this, plot and consider the plot of the posterior distribution of δ. Consider how much of the posterior distribution is such that |δ|>0.10 relative to small effect sizes (|δ|≤0.10). Of course, this glass is still mostly empty as the required sample sizes are still quite large. plot(density(posterior_d(d = 0.15, n1=15, n2=15, filter=0)), main=NA) Using Generic t or F-tests to compute expected power. Often effect size estimates are not presented in papers. Or the effect size estimate may not be standard or its calculation may be uncertain (and not match your own calculation based on the test statistic). For that reason fabs has functions that only require you to input the original test statistic and df and obviate the need to do the conversion to the effect size. That is done internally (r for the t-test and Cohen’s f for the F-test). The defaults are random predictors for the t-test and fixed predictors for the F-test. You want to replicate a study that observed t(53) = 2.75. What sample size will you need in the replication to have power of .80 assuming both the estimate and the future study have fixed predictors and you want to apply full publication bias filter (i.e., you would never have seen this study if it were not significant)? desired_ep_t(t = 2.75, df = 53, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=1, estimate_fixed=TRUE, future_fixed=TRUE) You are reviewing a manuscript examining an ANOVA with three groups where the authors claim that 40 per group will provide substantial statistical power (> .80) given previous research which yielded F(2, 24) = 4.20. Does this argument hold? desired_ep_Ftest(Ftest = 4.20, df1 = 2, df2=24, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=0, estimate_fixed=TRUE, future_fixed=TRUE) desired_ep_Ftest(Ftest = 4.20, df1 = 2, df2=24, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=1, estimate_fixed=TRUE, future_fixed=TRUE) 2

Sanity check As the precision of the effect size estimate increases, the expected power calculations for fixed predictors will converge to that of the traditional power calculation. To confirm this, examine expected power from extremely large sample t and the F-tests and compare these calculations to traditional power calculations. Standardized Mean Difference The result from power.t.test should match expected power when the estimate is extremely precise. Like when the sample size is 10,000,000 per group. power.t.test(delta = .50, sd = 1, sig.level=.05, power = .80, type="two.sample", alternative="two.sided")$n ## [1] 63.76576 # With extremely large samples, the variance in the posterior is essentially nil # and the result should correspond very closely to that from power.t.test() desired_ep_d(d = .50, n1=10000000, n2=10000000, desired_ep = 0.80, filter=0) F-test Consider Cohen’s f = 0.25 based on 4 groups (one-way between-subjects ANOVA). G*Power estimates that a total sample size of 180 will produce statistical power of 0.8039869. Now consider a very precisely estimated effect size fˆ=0.25 with df =3 and df =1000000. This corresponds to an observed F-test of 1 2 F(3,1000000)= df2×fˆ2 = 1000000×0.252 =20833.33. If we specify desired_ep = 0.8039869 then we should df1 3 observe a required sample size very, very close to 180. desired_ep_Ftest(Ftest = 20833.33, df1=3, df2=1000000, desired_ep = 0.8039869, filter=0, estimate_fixed=TRUE, future_fixed=TRUE) 3
